s: A 150-foot mosaic sea serpent built by 1,000 neighbours — and named for a dissident
w: Chilean artist Pedro Silva recruited roughly a thousand locals to paint individual mosaic tiles, which he assembled into a 150-foot serpentine dragon installed here in 1980. The park itself was named for Fannie Mae Dees, who placed a coffin on her lawn in protest when the city seized this land for urban renewal — she died before the park opened, having lost. Silva hid a portrait of her face among the tiles.
_facts: Chilean mosaic artist Pedro Silva created the 'Dragon' sculpture in 1980 with around 1,000 community volunteers.
Fannie Mae Dees protested the urban-renewal land seizure with a coffin on her lawn; the park was named in her honour posthumously.
Silva included a portrait of Dees herself among the mosaic tiles on the sculpture.
Restored in 2017-2018 by the Hillsboro-West End Neighborhood Association.

s: Two plaster polar bears born to sell frozen custard in 1930 — still here
w: G. Mattei Plaster Relief Ornamental Co. cast these bears in 1930 for a Polar Bear Frozen Custard shop on West End. Both shops closed during the Depression. In 1940 Reverend Zema Hill, a funeral-home owner, bought the bears and planted two outside his Edgehill Avenue home. They outlasted the house and in 2005 MDHA installed them in a community plaza at 12th and Edgehill, where they now serve as the neighbourhood's unofficial mascots.
_facts: Cast in 1930 by G. Mattei Plaster Relief Ornamental Co. as advertisements for Polar Bear Frozen Custard shops.
Purchased around 1940 by Reverend Zema Hill, a funeral home owner, who displayed them at 1408 Edgehill Avenue.
MDHA created a permanent community plaza to house the bears, unveiled August 2005.
Now a recognised neighbourhood symbol; two bears at the corner of 12th Ave South and Edgehill Avenue.

s: Nashville's first civil rights public art — hands and faces pressed into curved concrete
w: Installed in 2016 beside the Metro Courthouse, these curved walls by landscape architect Walter Hood render the 1960 civil rights movement in shadow graphics — hands registering to vote, students at lunch counters, marchers three abreast. The Courthouse steps opposite are where Diane Nash confronted Mayor Ben West in April 1960, forcing him to admit segregation was wrong. Three thousand people marched here in silence the morning after Z. Alexander Looby's house was bombed.
_facts: Designed by landscape architect Walter Hood; Nashville's first public artwork dedicated to the civil rights movement.
Installed 2016 at the northwest corner of Public Square Park beside the Metro Nashville Courthouse.
Depicts scenes from the 1960 Nashville sit-ins, school desegregation, Freedom Rides, and the April 1960 mass march.
On April 19, 1960, 3,000 protesters marched silently to this courthouse after Z. Alexander Looby's house was bombed.

s: The concrete steps are all that remain of the house bombed in 1960 — and they still stand
w: Before dawn on April 19, 1960, a bomb destroyed the front of the Meharry Boulevard home of Z. Alexander Looby, the attorney defending students arrested in Nashville's lunch-counter sit-ins. The blast also shattered 140 windows at Meharry Medical College across the street. Looby and his wife survived. By noon, 3,000 students were marching in silence to City Hall, where Mayor Ben West publicly admitted segregation was wrong — a turning point that led to desegregation three weeks later. The house is gone; the concrete front steps remain.
_facts: Bombed before dawn April 19, 1960; Z. Alexander Looby was the civil rights attorney defending sit-in protestors.
The blast destroyed the house and blew out 140 windows at Meharry Medical College across the street.
Triggered a silent 3,000-person march to City Hall, forcing Mayor Ben West to condemn segregation publicly.
Nashville desegregated its lunch counters on May 10, 1960 — the first major southern city to do so.
Only the concrete front steps of the house survive at 2012 Meharry Boulevard.

s: Hank Snow, Roy Acuff, Keith Whitley — country music's hall of fame underground
w: Forty thousand graves on land that began as a Presbyterian churchyard in 1785. Country music made this its own: Roy Acuff, Floyd Cramer, Hank Snow, Keith Whitley, John Hartford, Pete Drake, and Johnny Wright are here, among dozens more who shaped the sound of Nashville. Less visited than the museum, more honest than the museum. A self-guided tour map is available at the office.
_facts: Originated as the burial ground for Spring Hill Presbyterian Church, established 1785; formally chartered 1888.
Holds over 40,000 graves on the grounds at 5110 Gallatin Pike South in Madison.
Notable burials include Roy Acuff, Hank Snow, Keith Whitley, Floyd Cramer, John Hartford, and Pete Drake.
Also contains graves of early Nashville settlers dating to the late 18th century.

s: Confederate Circle, Thomas Ryman, and the first American woman pilot killed on duty
w: Laid out in 1856 after the manner of Boston's Mount Auburn, this 206-acre Victorian necropolis holds Thomas Ryman (the Ryman Auditorium's builder) and Cornelia Fort — the first American woman in US history killed on duty as a pilot, during a midair collision in 1943. At the cemetery's centre lies Confederate Circle: 1,500 soldiers buried in 13 concentric rows around a granite obelisk topped by a stone Confederate soldier, dedicated 1869.
_facts: Established 1856 by Adrian Van Sinderen Lindsley; modelled on Mount Auburn Cemetery in Cambridge, Massachusetts.
Thomas Ryman (builder of the Ryman Auditorium) is buried here.
Cornelia Fort, first American woman killed on duty as a military pilot (1943), is interred here.
Confederate Circle contains approximately 1,500 soldiers buried in 13 rows around an 1889 granite obelisk.

s: The first medical school for Black Americans in the South, founded 1876 in a church basement
w: Classes began in 1876 in the basement of Clark Memorial United Methodist Church, with two faculty and 11 students. The college was named for Samuel Meharry, an Irish-American immigrant whose family donated the funds, and was founded to train physicians for communities that had none. The blast from the 1960 Looby bombing — whose target lived across the street — blew out 140 Meharry windows. Today it remains the largest private HBCU focused solely on healthcare education in the United States.
_facts: Founded 1876 as the Medical Department of Central Tennessee College; first medical school for African Americans in the South.
Named for Samuel Meharry and his brothers, Irish-American immigrants who provided founding donations.
Chartered as an independent institution in 1915.
The Looby House bombing of April 1960 blew out 140 Meharry windows; the school is at 1005 Dr. D.B. Todd Jr. Boulevard.

s: A 150-foot portrait of a 91-year-old local painted on a derelict grain silo
w: In 2017 Australian artist Guido van Helten painted an enormous monochrome portrait on the side of a disused Gillette Grain Company silo in The Nations. The subject is Lee 'LD' Estes, a 91-year-old who had lived in the neighbourhood since the 1920s. Van Helten met him walking the streets during his site visit. The silo, then slated for demolition, became the centrepiece of the Silo Bend mixed-use development instead — the mural having made it locally irreplaceable.
_facts: Painted in spring 2017 by Australian street artist Guido van Helten on a disused Gillette Grain Company silo.
The subject, Lee 'LD' Estes, was a 91-year-old lifelong resident of The Nations neighbourhood.
The silo became the centrepiece of the Silo Bend development at 1407 51st Avenue North.
Commissioned by Southeast Venture in partnership with Nashville Walls Project.

s: A 1780 log stockade replica on the Cumberland bluff — where Nashville began
w: On January 1, 1780 James Robertson led settlers across a frozen Cumberland River to a bluff the Shawnee called French Lick. Within weeks they had built Fort Nashborough: a square of 20 log cabins surrounded by a palisade. The fort withstood a Cherokee attack in 1781 and was abandoned by 1794 as the town grew. The present replica at First Avenue North opened in 2017 as an interpretive centre on the river's edge, a few hundred metres from the original site. Free to enter.
_facts: Original fort established January 1780 by James Robertson and John Donelson; named for General Francis Nash.
Attacked by Cherokee forces in 1781 in the Battle of the Bluffs; abandoned by 1794.
The original 1930s WPA replica was demolished in 2015; a new interpretive centre opened July 2017 at 170 1st Avenue North.
The 1780 Cumberland Compact, an early governing document, was drafted at this settlement.

s: A Gothic Revival church on Music Row where Tennessee Williams spent his boyhood
w: The Church of the Advent was built in 1911; its rector from 1914, Rev. W.E. Dakin, was the maternal grandfather of playwright Tennessee Williams, who reportedly lived in the rectory as a child. The church became the Tony Alamo ministry's headquarters in the early 1990s, then in 1996 was converted by Allen Sides and Gary Belz into Ocean Way Nashville, one of the finest orchestral recording studios in the eastern United States. Belmont University has owned it since 2001.
_facts: The 1911 Church of the Advent's rector from 1914, Rev. W.E. Dakin, was Tennessee Williams's maternal grandfather.
Tony Alamo's ministry used the building in the early 1990s.
Converted to Ocean Way Nashville recording studio in 1996 by Allen Sides and Gary Belz.
Belmont University purchased the studio in 2001; it remains one of the premier orchestral recording facilities in the US at 1200 17th Avenue South.

s: The 11th President is buried on the Capitol lawn — after two earlier graves elsewhere
w: James K. Polk died only 103 days after leaving office in 1849, aged 53, of cholera caught on a goodwill tour of the South. He was first buried in Nashville City Cemetery, then moved to the grounds of his Polk Place estate, then moved again when Polk Place was demolished in 1901. His neoclassical tomb — four columns supporting a small canopy — has sat on the Capitol's east garden since 1893, sharing the grounds with the building whose architect is entombed in the northeast wall.
_facts: James K. Polk died June 15, 1849 — just 103 days after leaving the presidency — of cholera.
Buried first in Nashville City Cemetery, then on his Polk Place estate, then moved to the Capitol grounds in 1893 when Polk Place was sold.
His wife Sarah Childress Polk is buried beside him; she died in 1891.
The neoclassical canopied tomb sits on the Capitol's east side, near the statue of Andrew Jackson, at 6th Avenue North.

s: A French-Canadian fur trader lived in this limestone cave above the Cumberland in the 1760s
w: Timothy Demonbreun — Jacques-Timothee Boucher Sieur de Montbrun — arrived at the salt licks the Shawnee called French Lick in 1766 and used this cave in the Cumberland River bluff as his base camp, spending months at a time inside. The cave is roughly 125 feet long, entered through a fissure about 20 feet above the waterline. Demonbreun is recognised as the first permanent European resident of what became Nashville; the street named for him runs through the city's entertainment district. One of only two caves on the National Register of Historic Places.
_facts: Used as a dwelling and camp by French-Canadian fur trader Timothy Demonbreun from around 1766.
Located in the south bluff of the Cumberland River, roughly 2.5 miles east of the courthouse.
Cave is approximately 125 feet long; entrance sits about 20 feet above the average waterline.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1979; one of only two caves on the NRHP.

s: A lily pond turned Japanese water garden, crossed by Tennessee's first concrete bridge
w: For the 1897 Tennessee Centennial Exposition this hollow was landscaped into Lily Lake. By 1922 it had become a Japanese Water Garden with aquatic plants and a tea-house atmosphere. The small bridge linking the sunken garden to Lake Watauga, built in 1906, is the first reinforced concrete bridge ever constructed in Tennessee. The Japanese garden was dismantled in 1949; the sunken plantings remain — one of the most quietly beautiful spots in Centennial Park and almost nobody knows the bridge is a record-setter.
_facts: The hollow was a pond called Lily Lake during the 1897 Tennessee Centennial Exposition.
Became a Japanese Water Garden displaying aquatic plants from 1922 to 1949.
The bridge connecting the sunken garden to Lake Watauga was built in 1906 and is the first reinforced concrete bridge in Tennessee.
Centennial Park itself opened in 1903 on the former 1897 exposition grounds.

s: The first concrete-pier arch bridge in North America — now the longest pedestrian bridge in the US
w: The Shelby Street Bridge was built in 1909 with a then-radical engineering first: concrete arched piers supporting a steel through-truss, a combination never before attempted in North America. Declared hazardous and scheduled for demolition in 1998, a preservation campaign converted it into a pedestrian and cycling span in 2003 — at 3,150 feet the longest pedestrian bridge in the United States. Renamed in 2014 for John Seigenthaler, the Nashville journalist who was beaten by a mob during the 1961 Freedom Rides.
_facts: Built 1909; first bridge in North America supported by arched concrete piers rather than masonry or steel.
Closed to vehicles in 1998 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places the same year.
Converted to a pedestrian and cycling bridge in 2003; at 3,150 feet the longest pedestrian bridge in the US.
Renamed in 2014 for journalist John Seigenthaler, who was beaten by a mob in Montgomery, Alabama during the 1961 Freedom Rides.

s: A Civil War-damaged Greek Revival mansion hidden inside a public park, almost nobody knows it's there
w: Built in the 1840s for Mary Childress Benton, whose brother-in-law Senator Thomas Hart Benton and brother Jesse both fought a pistol duel with Andrew Jackson in 1813, this Greek Revival house was ransacked during the Civil War. It passed through several Nashville families before Granville Sevier renovated it in 1927; his heirs gave it to the city in 1945. It now sits inside Sevier Park, unmarked from the street, housing the Metropolitan Historical Commission. The park's dog walkers mostly have no idea what they're walking past.
_facts: Built c.1840s in Greek Revival style for Mary Childress Benton, sister-in-law of Senator Thomas Hart Benton.
The Benton brothers (Jesse and Thomas Hart) fought a pistol brawl with Andrew Jackson in Nashville in 1813.
Damaged during the Civil War Battle of Nashville; later purchased by the Sevier family in 1927.
Donated to the City of Nashville in 1945; listed on the National Register of Historic Places 1974.
